Title: Acclimation of metabolism to light in Arabidopsis thaliana: the glucose 6-phosphate/phosphate translocator GPT2 directs metabolic acclimation

Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: plastidic phosphate translocator family; glucose-6-phosphate/phosphate antiporter
Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: starch and sucrose metabolism; primary carbon metabolism; chloroplast-cytosol carbohydrate partitioning
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: hexose phosphate transport step linking cytosolic sucrose metabolism to chloroplast starch synthesis
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: transport-linked branch point between cytosolic hexose phosphate pool and chloroplast starch synthesis
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: glucose 6-phosphate translocated into chloroplast with reciprocal phosphate exchange
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Exchanges glucose 6-phosphate and inorganic phosphate across the chloroplast envelope; the paper proposes net import of cytosolic glucose 6-phosphate into the chloroplast during high-light acclimation.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Rapidly induced by high light in mature leaves; previously reported to increase about 20-fold within 2 h after transfer to high light
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Low under low light; strongly induced during high-light acclimation in mature leaves
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Transfer from low light (100 μmol m−2 s−1) to high light (400 μmol m−2 s−1) in mature leaves
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Transient carbon reserve that buffers carbon supply during light acclimation
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes starch accumulation and enables acclimation of photosynthetic capacity during transfer to high light; loss of GPT2 causes lower starch accumulation and exaggerated accumulation of sugar phosphates including G6P and T6P.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. GPT2 is required for dynamic acclimation of photosynthetic capacity in mature Arabidopsis leaves after transfer from low to high light. 2. Wild type accumulated more starch than gpt2 knockout plants throughout acclimation, despite similar sucrose accumulation. 3. gpt2 mutants showed higher levels of sugar phosphates and altered metabolomic/transcriptomic responses, supporting a role for GPT2 in buffering metabolite distribution between cytosol and chloroplast. 4. The authors propose that GPT2 mediates net import of G6P into chloroplasts, increasing starch synthesis under high light.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: T-DNA knockout mutant analysis (gpt2.2 and supporting gpt2.1); photosynthetic gas exchange; starch and sucrose assays; targeted LC-MS/MS of sugar phosphates; global metabolomics (GC-MS, FTIR); microarray transcriptomics
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control step for metabolic acclimation and chloroplast-cytosol carbon partitioning during high-light acclimation; not established as a classic rate-limiting enzyme
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Affymetrix ATH1 microarray; GC-EI-TOF/MS metabolomics; LC-MS/MS targeted metabolite profiling; accession NA
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: gpt2 loss abolishes photosynthetic acclimation and reduces starch accumulation under high light; no obvious differences in low-light growth, morphology, seed yield, photosynthesis or respiration were noted
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Target for engineering improved acclimation to fluctuating/high light by enhancing chloroplast-cytosol carbon partitioning and transient starch formation; should be considered together with transport/signaling networks controlling carbon homeostasis
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Although GPT2 is a transporter rather than a catalyst, under the requested scheme it is best placed as a pathway gene because it directly mediates the metabolic step central to the paper. The paper emphasizes source and applied species are the same. The authors infer net G6P import into chloroplasts from physiological and metabolite evidence rather than direct flux measurement.
